reason: Elasticity is the cloud property of automatically scaling resources up/down with demand; virtualization, encryption and metering do not adjust capacity.
A computer network is a set of interconnected devices that share resources and information; 'standalone computers' is the negation of a network.
Nodes, transmission media and networking devices are core network terminology; operating system is a general computing concept.
A router interconnects distinct networks and forwards/routes packets toward their destination; A describes a switch, B a modem, D an access point.
Gmail is a ready-to-use hosted application, i.e. Software as a Service.
LAN covers a small area such as a home, office or building; PAN is a much smaller personal range (a few metres), so LAN is the intended answer.
TCP/IP = Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol, the suite that packetizes and delivers data across the internet.
A hub is a dumb repeater that floods frames out every port, unlike a switch which forwards by MAC address.
Hybrid cloud is a deployment model combining public and private cloud.
Multi-cloud means consuming services from more than one cloud provider.
AWS, Azure and Google Cloud are providers; 'Oracle Database' is a DBMS product (the provider would be Oracle Cloud/OCI).
In P2P there is no dedicated server: every peer can act as both client and server.
Pay-as-you-go bills strictly by actual consumption/usage.
Security and protection of data/resources is a standard stated goal; the other three options are anti-goals.
PaaS supplies runtime, middleware and tooling so developers deploy code without managing infrastructure.
A PAN connects an individual's personal devices over a very short range (e.g. Bluetooth).
Worst case pivot is the extreme element, giving subproblems of size 0 and n-1: T(n)=T(n-1)+O(n) = O(n^2).
Inner loop runs i times while i halves each pass: n + n/2 + n/4 + ... = 2n, so O(n).
Merge sort is O(n log n) worst case; bubble, quick and selection are all O(n^2) worst case.
All-pairs shortest paths (Floyd-Warshall) is the classic dynamic programming algorithm.
